title: What indexing strategies help vector databases perform similarity search efficiently at scale?
prompt:
What indexing strategies help vector databases perform similarity search efficiently at scale?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
Exact k-NN search is O(N × D) and far too slow for real-time AI at scale — ANN (approximate nearest neighbor) algorithms structure the vector space for logarithmic-ish navigation instead. HNSW builds multi-layer navigable small world graphs and is the standard choice for RAG up to roughly 10M-50M vectors, offering the highest recall of the three. IVF uses k-means to build inverted Voronoi cluster centroids, and is a good fit for fast indexing when memory is constrained. IVF-PQ combines inverted clusters with product quantization and is what you reach for at 100M+ to billion-scale vector collections. Critical HNSW tuning parameters: `ef_construction` trades index quality against build time, `ef_search` trades query recall against search latency, and `M` controls graph edge density per node.

**Takeaway:** HNSW is the default for RAG-scale collections (up to tens of millions of vectors); reach for IVF or IVF-PQ specifically when memory constraints or billion-scale collection sizes rule HNSW out.
hint_notes: HNSW = best recall, up to ~50M vectors. IVF = memory-constrained fast indexing. IVF-PQ = billion-scale. Tune HNSW via ef_construction (build), ef_search (query recall/latency), M (graph density).
